result:
The rhythmic clack of the wheels against the rails was a metronome counting down seconds that no longer mattered. Elias sat in the velvet-upholstered booth, his forehead pressed against the cool glass of the window. Outside, the world was a smudge of charcoal and indigo. There were no stars, no moon, just the endless, rolling fog that seemed to chase the train, swallowing the tracks behind it as soon as they were laid. He didn’t remember buying a ticket. He didn’t remember boarding. He only knew the heavy ache in his chest and the certainty that he was heading somewhere inevitable. The air in the carriage smelled of old tobacco and polished brass.

The carriage was almost empty, a purgatory of worn velvet and scarred wood. At the far end, an old woman knitted a scarf from yarn the color of dried blood, her needles clicking a sharp counter-rhythm to the train. Across the aisle, a young man in a rumpled suit slept with his mouth slightly open, a half-finished crossword puzzle resting on his knee. Elias looked down at his own hands. They were clean, pale, lacking the usual smudges of ink and grease that came from fixing antique clocks. He patted his pockets. No wallet, no keys, no phone. Just a small, heavy piece of paper in his breast pocket. He didn't pull it out. He wasn't ready to see the destination.

The door at the end of the carriage slid open with a pneumatic hiss, and the Conductor stepped through. He wore a midnight-blue uniform, brass buttons dulled by countless journeys. His face was lined with the deep grooves of a man who had seen too many departures. He didn’t check on the sleeping man or the knitting woman. He stopped right beside Elias’s seat.

"Tickets," the Conductor said. His voice was like gravel shifting in a stream.

Elias hesitated, then pulled the slip of paper from his pocket. It was thick, like cardstock, but entirely blank. No origin, no destination, no price. Just a blank space.

The Conductor took it, looked at it, and nodded slowly. "Ah. The Waiting Place."

"I don't know what that means," Elias said, his voice raspy from disuse.

"You will. You'll know when the fog lifts." The Conductor punched a single hole in the blank card and handed it back. "We arrive at dawn. Try to get some rest."

The Conductor moved on, disappearing through the door at the front of the carriage. Elias stared at the punched hole. A perfect circle of absence. He felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to run, to pull the emergency cord, to stop this relentless momentum. But where would he run to? The fog outside was impenetrable. He was suspended in a bubble of moving light, hurtling through a void. He leaned back and closed his eyes, letting the rocking of the train soothe the panic in his ribs. He thought of his shop, the hundreds of ticking clocks. He had spent his life listening to the mechanical heartbeats of others, ignoring his own erratic rhythm. 

He woke to the smell of coffee. The carriage was bathed in a soft, morning glow. The sleeping man was gone, his crossword left behind, completely filled out with black ink. The old woman was gone, but the red scarf was draped over the armrest of her seat. Elias stood, his joints stiff, and walked down the narrow corridor toward the dining car. The floor swayed gently beneath his feet, demanding a constant, subtle adjustment of balance.

The dining car was a vision of an era he had only seen in photographs. Art deco lamps hung from the ceiling, casting prisms of light across polished mahogany tables. Silver cutlery gleamed on white linen, though there was no one to use it. A man in a white linen suit sat at a table near the window, sipping from a delicate porcelain cup. He looked up as Elias entered and smiled, gesturing to the seat across from him.

"Sit," the man said. "The coffee is surprisingly good for the end of the world."

Elias sat. "Is that where we are? The end of the world?"

The man shrugged. "An end. A beginning. A threshold. Take your pick." He poured Elias a cup from a silver pot. The steam rose in lazy spirals. "I’m Arthur, by the way."

"Elias." He took a sip. It was bitter, hot, and painfully real. "I don't remember how I got here."

"We rarely do," Arthur said, looking out the window. The fog was beginning to thin, revealing shadows of a landscape—twisted trees, crumbling ruins, rivers that flowed upward into the sky. "We only remember the weight we carried. The clocks we couldn't fix."

Elias flinched. "How do you know about the clocks?"

"Because I was a watchmaker," Arthur said. "I spent forty years trying to capture time in tiny brass cages. Then I realized time didn't want to be caught. It wanted to move, to break, to flow away. I spent so long holding onto the ticking that I forgot to live in the spaces between the seconds."

Elias stared out. The twisted trees looked like agonized hands. He thought of the loneliness of his shop, the desperate need to keep the gears turning just to prove he existed. 

"I was afraid of the silence," Elias whispered.

"Silence is just the space where the next note is born," Arthur said. He stood, buttoning his linen jacket. "I’m getting off at the next stop. The waiting is over for me."

"What's out there?"

Arthur looked at him with eyes that held a thousand years of quiet acceptance. "Whatever you need it to be. A quiet beach. A bustling city. A blank page." He tipped his head. "Good luck, Elias. Let the clock stop."

Arthur walked out of the dining car. A moment later, the train began to slow. The brakes shrieked, a terrible, grinding sound of metal yielding to inevitability. Elias gripped the edge of the table. The fog outside shattered like spun glass.

Suddenly, there was light. Blinding, searing, beautiful light. It flooded the carriage, burning away the shadows, the dust, the lingering scent of old coffee and polished wood. Elias looked down at his hands. They were fading, becoming translucent, the outline of the seat visible through his fingers. The panic returned, sharp and sudden, but beneath it was something else. A profound relief. 

He didn’t have to wind the springs anymore. He didn’t have to count the seconds. 

The train ground to a halt. There was no station platform, no bustling crowd, no conductors with midnight uniforms. There was only a door that swung open, letting in a breeze that smelled of salt, rain, and fresh paper. 

Elias stood up. He left the coffee cup on the table, half-empty. He walked down the corridor, past the empty seats, past the red scarf and the blacked-out crossword, and stepped off the train. His feet met soft sand. The light wrapped around him like a warm coat. Behind him, the train hissed, its doors closing, and it pulled away into the bright, formless distance, carrying the ghosts of the waiting place with it.

Elias stood alone on the shore of an endless sea, the silence ringing in his ears. For the first time in his life, it didn't sound like an ending. It sounded like an invitation.

message.reasoning:
1.  **Analyze the Request:**
    *   Constraint: Exactly or approximately 1000 words (usually "you have 1000 words" implies a target, not a strict exactness, but hitting close to the mark is good practice). Let's aim for a rich, well-paced narrative or essay that feels substantial.
    *   Prompt: "Write whatever comes to you." Complete creative freedom.
    *   Goal: Produce something compelling, evocative, and well-structured within the word count.

2.  **Brainstorming Core Concepts:**
    *   *Idea 1:* A sci-fi piece about the last archivist on a dying space station. (A bit cliché).
    *   *Idea 2:* A philosophical essay on the nature of time and memory. (A bit dry).
    *   *Idea 3:* A magical realism piece about a town where it rains objects instead of water, focusing on a specific character's experience. (Intriguing, visual).
    *   *Idea 4:* A grounded, emotional story about a lighthouse keeper and a message in a bottle, bridging the past and present. (Classic, resonant, allows for good atmospheric writing).

3.  **Selecting and Refining the Concept (Idea 4):**
    Let's go with the lighthouse, but twist it slightly. Not a lighthouse, but a radio operator in a remote cabin, listening to the static of the world, waiting for a specific frequency. Let's make it about a woman named Elara who repairs old analog radios. The radios hold the echoes of the past. She's trying to reconstruct a specific voice.

    *Wait, let's pivot to something more universal and atmospheric.* Let's write about the concept of "The Waiting Place"—a metaphorical space between what was and what will be. A train station at the edge of a vast, quiet desert.
    Let's do a narrative about a train station. The Midnight Express. A passenger who doesn't know where they are going, only that they had to leave.

4.  **Structuring the Narrative:**
    *   **Introduction (approx. 150 words):** Establish the setting. The train compartment. The rhythmic sound of the tracks. The protagonist's lack of memory or destination.
    *   **Rising Action (approx. 300 words):** The interaction with the environment. The other passengers. The dining car. The feeling of liminality. Meeting a fellow traveler who offers a piece of wisdom or a shared sense of displacement.
    *   **Climax (approx. 300 words):** Looking out the window. The landscape shifts from familiar to surreal. A realization about what the train actually is—a vessel for transition, for shedding the past. The ticket inspection.
    *   **Falling Action (approx. 150 words):** The acceptance of the journey. The fading of anxiety. The approaching light of a new dawn or a new station.
    *   **Resolution (approx. 100 words):** Stepping off the train. The feeling of readiness.

5.  **Drafting - Section by Section (monitoring word count):**

    *   *Intro:* The rhythmic clack of the wheels against the rails was a metronome counting down seconds that no longer mattered. Elias sat in the velvet-upholstered booth, his forehead pressed against the cool glass of the window. Outside, the world was a smudge of charcoal and indigo. There were no stars, no moon, just the endless, rolling fog that seemed to chase the train. He didn’t remember buying a ticket. He didn’t remember boarding. He only knew the heavy ache in his chest and the certainty that he was heading somewhere inevitable.

    *   *Developing the scene:* The carriage was almost empty. At the far end, an old woman knitted a scarf from yarn the color of dried blood, her needles clicking a counter-rhythm to the train. Across the aisle, a young man in a rumpled suit slept with his mouth slightly open, a half-finished crossword puzzle resting on his knee. Elias looked down at his own hands. They were clean, pale, lacking the usual smudges of ink and grease that came from fixing antique clocks. He patted his pockets. No wallet, no keys, no phone. Just a small, heavy piece of paper in his breast pocket. He didn't pull it out. He wasn't ready to see the destination.

    *   *Interaction:* The door at the end of the carriage slid open with a pneumatic hiss, and the Conductor stepped through. He wore a uniform of deep midnight blue, the brass buttons dulled by time. He didn’t check on the sleeping man or the knitting woman. He stopped right beside Elias’s seat.
    "Tickets," the Conductor said. His voice was like gravel shifting in a stream.
    Elias hesitated, then pulled the slip of paper from his pocket. It was thick, like cardstock, but entirely blank. No origin, no destination, no price. Just a blank space.
    The Conductor took it, looked at it, and nodded. "Ah. The Waiting Place."
    "I don't know what that means," Elias said, his voice raspy from disuse.
    "You will. You'll know when the fog lifts." The Conductor punched a single hole in the blank card and handed it back. "We arrive at dawn. Try to get some rest."

    *   *Deepening the metaphor:* The Conductor moved on, disappearing through the door at the front of the carriage. Elias stared at the punched hole. A perfect circle of absence. He felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to run, to pull the emergency cord, to stop this relentless momentum. But where would he run to? The fog outside was impenetrable. He was suspended in a bubble of moving light, hurtling through a void. He leaned back and closed his eyes, letting the rocking of the train soothe the panic in his ribs.

    *   *A shift:* He woke to the smell of coffee. The carriage was bathed in a soft, amber glow. The sleeping man was gone, his crossword left behind, completely filled out with black ink. The old woman was gone, but the red scarf was draped over the armrest of her seat. Elias stood, his joints stiff, and walked down the narrow corridor toward the dining car.

    *   *The Dining Car:* It was a vision of an era he had only seen in photographs. Art deco lamps hung from the ceiling, casting prisms of light across polished mahogany tables. A man in a white linen suit sat at a table near the window, sipping from a delicate porcelain cup. He looked up as Elias entered and smiled, gesturing to the seat across from him.

    "Because I was a watchmaker," Arthur said softly. "In another life. I spent forty years trying to capture time in tiny brass cages. And then one day, I realized time didn't want to be caught. He thought of his shop, the hundreds of ticking clocks, the silence that descended only when he closed the doors at night. He thought of the loneliness of it, the desperate need to keep things moving, to keep the gears turning, just to prove he existed.

    Arthur looked at him with eyes that held a thousand years of quiet acceptance. "Whatever you need it to be. A quiet beach. A bustling city. A blank page." He tipped his head. "Good luck, Elias. Let the clock stop."

    Suddenly, there was light. Blinding, searing, beautiful light. It flooded the carriage, burning away the shadows, the dust, the lingering scent of old coffee and polished wood. Elias looked down at his hands. They were fading, becoming translucent, the outline of the seat visible through his fingers. The panic returned, sharp and sudden, but beneath it was something else. A profound relief. 

    He didn’t have to wind the springs anymore. He didn’t have to count the seconds. 

    *   *Resolution:* The train ground to a halt. There was no station platform, no bustling crowd, no conductors with midnight uniforms. There was only a door that swung open, letting in a breeze that smelled of salt, rain, and fresh paper. 

    Elias stood up. He left the coffee cup on the table, half-empty. He walked down the corridor, past the empty seats, past the red scarf and the blacked-out crossword, and stepped off the train. His feet met soft sand. The light wrapped around him like a warm coat. Behind him, the train hissed, its doors closing, and it pulled away into the bright, formless distance, carrying the ghosts of the waiting place with it.

    Elias stood alone on the shore of an endless sea, the silence ringing in his ears. For the first time in his life, it didn't sound like an ending. It sounded like an invitation.

6.  **Word Count Check & Expansion/Contraction:**
    Let's estimate the current length. It looks to be around 900-950 words. I want to make sure it hits the 1000-word mark comfortably without padding. I'll flesh out the sensory details, the internal monologue of Elias, and the descriptions of the liminal spaces to ensure a rich texture and accurate length.

    *Expansion points:*
    - Describe the train carriage more deeply at the start. The smell, the dust motes, the temperature.
    - Elaborate on the Conductor's appearance. The brass buttons, the aura of ancient patience.
    - Expand Elias's memory of the clock shop. The specific weight of the brass, the sound of a thousand heartbeats.
    - Enhance the dining car scene. The clinking of silverware that isn't there, the reflection in the glass.
    - Deepen the conversation with Arthur. More about the nature of holding on vs. letting go.

7.  **Drafting - Second Pass (incorporating expansions):**
    (Writing out the full 1000-word text based on the refined structure and expansion points. I will count words carefully to ensure I land right around the 1000-word target.)

    *Paragraph 1:* The rhythmic clack of the wheels against the rails was a metronome counting down seconds that no longer mattered. Elias sat in the velvet-upholstered booth, his forehead pressed against the cool glass of the window. Outside, the world was a smudge of charcoal and indigo. reasoning_details.text:
1.  **Analyze the Request:**
    *   Constraint: Exactly or approximately 1000 words (usually "you have 1000 words" implies a target, not a strict exactness, but hitting close to the mark is good practice). Let's aim for a rich, well-paced narrative or essay that feels substantial.
    *   Prompt: "Write whatever comes to you." Complete creative freedom.
    *   Goal: Produce something compelling, evocative, and well-structured within the word count.

2.  **Brainstorming Core Concepts:**
    *   *Idea 1:* A sci-fi piece about the last archivist on a dying space station. (A bit cliché).
    *   *Idea 2:* A philosophical essay on the nature of time and memory. (A bit dry).
    *   *Idea 3:* A magical realism piece about a town where it rains objects instead of water, focusing on a specific character's experience. (Intriguing, visual).
    *   *Idea 4:* A grounded, emotional story about a lighthouse keeper and a message in a bottle, bridging the past and present. (Classic, resonant, allows for good atmospheric writing).

3.  **Selecting and Refining the Concept (Idea 4):**
    Let's go with the lighthouse, but twist it slightly. Not a lighthouse, but a radio operator in a remote cabin, listening to the static of the world, waiting for a specific frequency. Let's make it about a woman named Elara who repairs old analog radios. The radios hold the echoes of the past. She's trying to reconstruct a specific voice.

    *Wait, let's pivot to something more universal and atmospheric.* Let's write about the concept of "The Waiting Place"—a metaphorical space between what was and what will be. A train station at the edge of a vast, quiet desert.
    Let's do a narrative about a train station. The Midnight Express. A passenger who doesn't know where they are going, only that they had to leave.

4.  **Structuring the Narrative:**
    *   **Introduction (approx. 150 words):** Establish the setting. The train compartment. The rhythmic sound of the tracks. The protagonist's lack of memory or destination.
    *   **Rising Action (approx. 300 words):** The interaction with the environment. The other passengers. The dining car. The feeling of liminality. Meeting a fellow traveler who offers a piece of wisdom or a shared sense of displacement.
    *   **Climax (approx. 300 words):** Looking out the window. The landscape shifts from familiar to surreal. A realization about what the train actually is—a vessel for transition, for shedding the past. The ticket inspection.
    *   **Falling Action (approx. 150 words):** The acceptance of the journey. The fading of anxiety. The approaching light of a new dawn or a new station.
    *   **Resolution (approx. 100 words):** Stepping off the train. The feeling of readiness.

5.  **Drafting - Section by Section (monitoring word count):**

    *   *Intro:* The rhythmic clack of the wheels against the rails was a metronome counting down seconds that no longer mattered. Elias sat in the velvet-upholstered booth, his forehead pressed against the cool glass of the window. Outside, the world was a smudge of charcoal and indigo. There were no stars, no moon, just the endless, rolling fog that seemed to chase the train. He didn’t remember buying a ticket. He didn’t remember boarding. He only knew the heavy ache in his chest and the certainty that he was heading somewhere inevitable.

    *   *Developing the scene:* The carriage was almost empty. At the far end, an old woman knitted a scarf from yarn the color of dried blood, her needles clicking a counter-rhythm to the train. Across the aisle, a young man in a rumpled suit slept with his mouth slightly open, a half-finished crossword puzzle resting on his knee. Elias looked down at his own hands. They were clean, pale, lacking the usual smudges of ink and grease that came from fixing antique clocks. He patted his pockets. No wallet, no keys, no phone. Just a small, heavy piece of paper in his breast pocket. He didn't pull it out. He wasn't ready to see the destination.

    *   *Interaction:* The door at the end of the carriage slid open with a pneumatic hiss, and the Conductor stepped through. He wore a uniform of deep midnight blue, the brass buttons dulled by time. He didn’t check on the sleeping man or the knitting woman. He stopped right beside Elias’s seat.
    "Tickets," the Conductor said. His voice was like gravel shifting in a stream.
    Elias hesitated, then pulled the slip of paper from his pocket. It was thick, like cardstock, but entirely blank. No origin, no destination, no price. Just a blank space.
    The Conductor took it, looked at it, and nodded. "Ah. The Waiting Place."
    "I don't know what that means," Elias said, his voice raspy from disuse.
    "You will. You'll know when the fog lifts." The Conductor punched a single hole in the blank card and handed it back. "We arrive at dawn. Try to get some rest."

    *   *Deepening the metaphor:* The Conductor moved on, disappearing through the door at the front of the carriage. Elias stared at the punched hole. A perfect circle of absence. He felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to run, to pull the emergency cord, to stop this relentless momentum. But where would he run to? The fog outside was impenetrable. He was suspended in a bubble of moving light, hurtling through a void. He leaned back and closed his eyes, letting the rocking of the train soothe the panic in his ribs.

    *   *A shift:* He woke to the smell of coffee. The carriage was bathed in a soft, amber glow. The sleeping man was gone, his crossword left behind, completely filled out with black ink. The old woman was gone, but the red scarf was draped over the armrest of her seat. Elias stood, his joints stiff, and walked down the narrow corridor toward the dining car.

    *   *The Dining Car:* It was a vision of an era he had only seen in photographs. Art deco lamps hung from the ceiling, casting prisms of light across polished mahogany tables. A man in a white linen suit sat at a table near the window, sipping from a delicate porcelain cup. He looked up as Elias entered and smiled, gesturing to the seat across from him.
    "Sit," the man said. "The coffee is surprisingly good for the end of the world."
    Elias sat. "Is that where we are? The end of the world?"
    The man shrugged. "An end. A beginning. A threshold. Take your pick." He poured Elias a cup from a silver pot. The steam rose in lazy spirals. "I’m Arthur, by the way."
    "Elias." He took a sip. It was bitter, hot, and painfully real. "I don't remember how I got here."
    "We rarely do," Arthur said, looking out the window. The fog was beginning to thin, revealing shadows of a landscape—twisted trees, crumbling ruins, rivers that flowed upward into the sky. "We only remember the weight we carried. The clocks we couldn't fix."

    *   *The Core Revelation:* Elias flinched. "How do you know about the clocks?"
    "Because I was a watchmaker," Arthur said softly. "In another life. I spent forty years trying to capture time in tiny brass cages. And then one day, I realized time didn't want to be caught. It wanted to move, to break, to flow away. I spent so long trying to hold onto the ticking that I forgot to live in the spaces between the seconds."
    Elias stared at the shifting landscape outside. The twisted trees looked like agonized hands reaching for a sky that wouldn't hold them. He thought of his shop, the hundreds of ticking clocks, the silence that descended only when he closed the doors at night. He thought of the loneliness of it, the desperate need to keep things moving, to keep the gears turning, just to prove he existed. 
    "I was afraid of the silence," Elias whispered.
    "Silence is just the space where the next note is born," Arthur said. He stood, buttoning his linen jacket. "I’m getting off at the next stop. The waiting is over for me."
    "What's out there?"
    Arthur looked at him with eyes that held a thousand years of quiet acceptance. "Whatever you need it to be. A quiet beach. A bustling city. A blank page." He tipped his head. "Good luck, Elias. Let the clock stop."

    *   *The Climax:* Arthur walked out of the dining car. A moment later, the train began to slow. The brakes shrieked, a terrible, grinding sound of metal yielding to inevitability. Elias gripped the edge of the table. The fog outside shattered like spun glass. 

    Suddenly, there was light. Blinding, searing, beautiful light. It flooded the carriage, burning away the shadows, the dust, the lingering scent of old coffee and polished wood. Elias looked down at his hands. They were fading, becoming translucent, the outline of the seat visible through his fingers. The panic returned, sharp and sudden, but beneath it was something else. A profound relief. 

    He didn’t have to wind the springs anymore. He didn’t have to count the seconds. 

    *   *Resolution:* The train ground to a halt. There was no station platform, no bustling crowd, no conductors with midnight uniforms. There was only a door that swung open, letting in a breeze that smelled of salt, rain, and fresh paper. 

    Elias stood up. He left the coffee cup on the table, half-empty. He walked down the corridor, past the empty seats, past the red scarf and the blacked-out crossword, and stepped off the train. His feet met soft sand. The light wrapped around him like a warm coat. Behind him, the train hissed, its doors closing, and it pulled away into the bright, formless distance, carrying the ghosts of the waiting place with it.

    Elias stood alone on the shore of an endless sea, the silence ringing in his ears. For the first time in his life, it didn't sound like an ending. It sounded like an invitation.

6.  **Word Count Check & Expansion/Contraction:**
    Let's estimate the current length. It looks to be around 900-950 words. I want to make sure it hits the 1000-word mark comfortably without padding. I'll flesh out the sensory details, the internal monologue of Elias, and the descriptions of the liminal spaces to ensure a rich texture and accurate length.

    *Expansion points:*
    - Describe the train carriage more deeply at the start. The smell, the dust motes, the temperature.
    - Elaborate on the Conductor's appearance. The brass buttons, the aura of ancient patience.
    - Expand Elias's memory of the clock shop. The specific weight of the brass, the sound of a thousand heartbeats.
    - Enhance the dining car scene. The clinking of silverware that isn't there, the reflection in the glass.
    - Deepen the conversation with Arthur. More about the nature of holding on vs. letting go.

7.  **Drafting - Second Pass (incorporating expansions):**
    (Writing out the full 1000-word text based on the refined structure and expansion points. I will count words carefully to ensure I land right around the 1000-word target.)

Elias sat in the velvet-upholstered booth, his forehead pressed against the cool glass of the window. Outside, the world was a smudge of charcoal and indigo. There were no stars, no moon, just the endless, rolling fog that seemed to chase the train, swallowing the tracks behind it as soon as they were laid. He didn’t remember buying a ticket. He didn’t remember boarding. He only knew the heavy ache in his chest and the certainty that he was heading somewhere inevitable. The air in the carriage smelled of old tobacco, polished brass, and the faint, sweet decay of forgotten memories. Dust motes danced in the dim, amber light of the overhead lamps.

    *Paragraph 2:* The carriage was almost empty, a purgatory of worn velvet and scarred wood. At the far end, an old woman knitted a scarf from yarn the color of dried blood, her needles clicking a sharp counter-rhythm to the train. Across the aisle, a young man in a rumpled suit slept with his mouth slightly open, a half-finished crossword puzzle resting on his knee. Elias looked down at his own hands. They were clean, pale, lacking the usual smudges of ink and grease that came from fixing antique clocks. He patted his pockets. No wallet, no keys, no phone. Just a small, heavy piece of paper in his breast pocket. He didn't pull it out. He wasn't ready to see the destination.

    *Paragraph 3:* The door at the end of the carriage slid open with a pneumatic hiss, and the Conductor stepped through. He wore a uniform of deep midnight blue, the brass buttons dulled by time and the weight of countless journeys. He stopped right beside Elias’s seat.
    "Tickets," the Conductor said. His voice was like gravel shifting in a stream.
    Elias hesitated, then pulled the slip of paper from his pocket. It was thick, like cardstock, but entirely blank. No origin, no destination, no price. Just a blank space.
    The Conductor took it, looked at it, and nodded slowly. "Ah. The Waiting Place."

It wanted to move, to break, to flow away. I spent so long trying to hold onto the ticking that I forgot to live in the spaces between the seconds."
